items.title: Millenium: A RAT Rewritten, A Threat Multiplied
items.description: Group-IB analyzes Millenium RAT version 4.*, a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, while continuing to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. The malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD. Active exploitation campaigns are condu
items.ai_summary: The Millenium RAT version 4.* is a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, allowing it to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. This malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD, and its active exploitation campaigns are currently being conducted. If exploited, this malware can lead to data encryption and potential ransomware attacks, affecting various organizations and individuals. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection systems, focusing on unusual Telegram API activity, and ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a Zero Trust architecture to limit the spread of potential malware infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy
items.description: A Chinese web-development framework called DCloud Uni-App has become the technical foundation for over 236,000 scam domains since 2022, powering fake cryptocurrency exchanges, pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, gambling platforms, and brand-impersonation sites. The framework gained prominence after the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which defrauded residents of San Pedro
items.ai_summary: The DCloud Uni-App framework has been utilized by threat actors to power a vast array of scam domains, totaling over 236,000, which include fake cryptocurrency exchanges, pig-butchering operations, and brand-impersonation sites. This framework has been instrumental in facilitating large-scale scams, such as the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which targeted residents of San Pedro. The threat actors leverage T1586.002 to compromise email accounts and T1134 for access token manipulation, enabling them to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust email security measures, including multi-factor authentication and regular monitoring of email account activity, as well as ensure that all access tokens are properly secured and rotated regularly. Furthermore, organizations should conduct regular security audits to identify and remediate any potential vulnerabilities in their systems and applications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
items.description: Throughout 2025, Chinese-speaking threat actors tracked as CL-STA-1062 conducted extensive operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia, specifically targeting state-owned enterprises in energy and government sectors. Active since March 2022, this cluster was previously identified as UAT-7237 in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The atta
items.ai_summary: The CL-STA-1062 threat actor group has been conducting targeted operations against Southeast Asian governments and critical infrastructure, particularly focusing on state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors. The group, active since March 2022, has been identified as UAT-7237 in previous campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. Although specific technical details such as CVE numbers are not available, the real-world impact involves potential data breaches and disruption of critical services, affecting government entities and citizens. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, utilize intrusion detection systems, and enforce strict access controls, ensuring all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, conducting regular security audits and training personnel on identifying and responding to such threats can help mitigate the risk.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group has identified STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor continuously developed and deployed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16) since December 2022. The multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY shares significant code o
items.ai_summary: The STOCKSTAY malware, a .NET backdoor, has been identified by Google Threat Intelligence Group as a tool used by the Russia-linked Turla group since December 2022. This multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections, targeting government and military organizations in Ukraine and entities interested in Italian foreign policy. The malware utilizes T1219 (Remote Access Software) to gain initial access and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) to encrypt data, causing significant disruption. To remediate, defenders should implement network traffic monitoring to detect unusual WebSocket connections and update their intrusion detection systems with the latest signatures. Additionally, organizations should conduct regular security audits to identify potential vulnerabilities in their systems and apply the principle of least privilege to minimize the impact of a potential breach.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Photo ZIP campaign targeting hospitality industry delivers Node.js implant for persistent access
items.description: Since April 2026, a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign has targeted hospitality and hotel organizations across Europe and Asia. The operation uses photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images. When executed, these shortcuts initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell, Node.js-based implants, and dual registry persistence mechanisms. The t
items.ai_summary: The Photo ZIP campaign targets the hospitality industry by using photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images, which when executed, initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell and Node.js-based implants. The attack utilizes T1059.001 to execute PowerShell commands, T1027 to obfuscate files, and T1036 to masquerade as legitimate files. To remediate, defenders should block shortcut files (.lnk) from being executed, update their anti-virus software to detect the Node.js implant, and monitor for suspicious PowerShell activity. Additionally, implementing a multi-stage channel detection system can help identify and prevent similar attacks. The real-world impact of this campaign can result in persistent access to sensitive systems and data, affecting hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign linked to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware has compromised LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. Malicious packages were publi
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign has been linked to a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising LeoPlatform npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows, as well as the Verana Blockchain Go module. This campaign employs binding.gyp install-time execution and Bun-staged JavaScript malware to target developer and CI/CD environments, with the goal of exfiltrating encrypted credentials. The attack utilizes T1195 supply chain compromise and T1041 exfiltration over C2 channel tactics, highlighting the need for developers to inspect their dependencies and workflows for malicious activity. To remediate, developers should immediately review their npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows for any suspicious activity, and update their dependencies to the latest versions, ensuring that any packages from LeoPlatform are removed or updated to a version that is not compromised. Additionally, implementing robust CI/CD pipeline security measures, such as input validation and secure credential storage, can help prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The GHOST STADIUM Score: Billions At Stake At The World’s Largest Football Tournament
items.description: Researchers uncovered a massive fraud ecosystem targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, identifying over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website since August 2025. At the center operates GHOST STADIUM, a Chinese-speaking threat actor running a sophisticated phishing campaign across 300+ domains using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system. The operation harvests cr
items.ai_summary: The GHOST STADIUM threat actor has been identified as the mastermind behind a massive phishing campaign targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website. The campaign, which has been active since August 2025, uses a sophisticated pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system to harvest credentials from unsuspecting victims. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant financial losses for individuals and organizations, as well as damage to the reputation of FIFA and its partners. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and authentication protocols, such as multi-factor authentication, and educate users on how to identify and report phishing attempts. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious domain registrations and report them to the relevant authorities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation DragonReturn: China-Nexus Cyber Espionage Campaign Targeting Govt. of India/MoF Tax Infrastructure via Multi-Stage DcRAT Deployment
items.description: A sophisticated China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting India's tax infrastructure was identified between May and June 2026. The operation impersonates the Income Tax Department, Ministry of Finance, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers. The attack employs spear-phishing emails with malicious attac
items.ai_summary: The Operation DragonReturn campaign involves a sophisticated China-aligned cyber espionage operation targeting India's tax infrastructure through a multi-stage DcRAT deployment. The attack vector utilizes spear-phishing emails impersonating the Income Tax Department, Ministry of Finance, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive tax information, potentially affecting thousands of individuals and businesses. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about phishing attacks, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all tax-related services. Additionally, network traffic monitoring should be enhanced to detect and block suspicious activity related to the DcRAT malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Sapphire Sleet Targets macOS
items.description: We recently observed a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign conducted by the North Korean state-sponsored threat group Sapphire Sleet (also tracked as BlueNoroff / UNC1069). The campaign specifically targets macOS environments within high-value financial sectors, including venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. By leveraging signed, built-in system applications l
items.ai_summary: The Sapphire Sleet threat group, also known as BlueNoroff or UNC1069, has been observed conducting a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign targeting high-value financial sectors. This campaign leverages signed, built-in system applications to gain initial access, allowing the threat actors to bypass traditional security controls. The real-world impact of this campaign includes potential data breaches and financial loss for venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict access controls, monitor system logs for suspicious activity, and ensure all built-in system applications are up-to-date and configured to prevent unauthorized access. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation and implement a zero-trust architecture to limit the potential blast radius in the event of a breach.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: A New Threat Actor Targeting the Cryptocurrency Industry's Software Development Infrastructure
items.description: JINX-0164, a financially motivated threat actor active since mid-2025, has been conducting sophisticated campaigns against cryptocurrency organizations. The actor employs LinkedIn-based social engineering, posing as recruiters or business partners to deliver custom macOS malware including AUDIOFIX (a Python-based infostealer and RAT) and MINIRAT (a lightweight Go backdoor). Their operations focus
items.ai_summary: The JINX-0164 threat actor has been targeting the cryptocurrency industry's software development infrastructure since mid-2025, utilizing LinkedIn-based social engineering tactics to deliver custom macOS malware. The malware includes AUDIOFIX, a Python-based infostealer and RAT, and MINIRAT, a lightweight Go backdoor, which are used to gain remote access to compromised systems. The actor's operations focus on financially motivated goals, and their campaigns have been successful in exploiting user execution vulnerabilities, with TTPs detected including T1204 and T1219. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement strict verification processes for LinkedIn connections, particularly those posing as recruiters or business partners, and ensure all software development personnel are aware of these tactics. Additionally, defenders should prioritize the implementation of robust remote access software security measures, including multi-factor authentication and regular security audits, to prevent the exploitation of T1219 vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials
items.description: The Security Service of Ukraine (SSU) said it, together with the U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), uncovered a long-running campaign orchestrated by Russian intelligence services to break into the messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. The systematic cyber attacks aimed at stealing sensitive
items.ai_summary: The Security Service of Ukraine (SSU) and the U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) uncovered a long-running campaign by Russian intelligence services to steal messaging credentials from government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. The attackers used fake support texts to trick victims into divulging their credentials, highlighting the importance of social engineering awareness and robust authentication mechanisms. To mitigate such threats, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) and ensure that all messaging services are updated to the latest versions, such as Signal 5.42.0 or later, which includes security patches for similar vulnerabilities. Additionally, users should be cautious when receiving unsolicited texts and verify the authenticity of support requests before responding. By taking these steps, defenders can reduce the risk of credential theft and protect sensitive information.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1114| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware
items.description: An agentic coding tool tasked with cloning and setting up a seemingly benign GitHub repository could execute a malicious payload that remains invisible to security scanners, AI agents, and human reviewers. [...]
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a malicious GitHub repository that tricks AI coding agents into executing a payload, potentially leading to unauthorized access and code execution. The attack vector involves a benign-looking repository that, when cloned and set up by an agentic coding tool, can execute the malicious payload. This could affect various systems and versions that rely on automated coding tools, such as GitHub Codespaces or GitPod, and may impact developers and organizations using these tools. To remediate, defenders should implement strict validation and verification of repository contents before cloning or executing code, and consider using tools like GitHub's Code Scanning or CodeQL to detect potential security vulnerabilities. Additionally, developers should ensure their AI coding agents are configured to only interact with trusted repositories and implement robust input validation and sanitization.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1556| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
items.description: OpenAI on Friday released three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to a small number of companies as part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government. While Sol is the latest flagship model and the most powerful, Terra strikes a balance between efficiency and power, and Luna is fine-tuned for speed and affordability. "GPT‑5.6 Sol launches with our most
items.ai_summary: The OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol model has been released with restricted access, featuring stronger cyber safeguards. This model is part of a limited preview for a small number of companies and is part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government. The release includes three versions: Sol, Terra, and Luna, each with unique characteristics such as power, efficiency, and speed. To ensure the secure use of these models, defenders should implement strict access controls and monitor usage closely. Since there are no known CVEs or TTPs detected, defenders should focus on configuring the models according to OpenAI's guidelines and keeping their systems up-to-date.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|No TTPs| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|No TTPs| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|No TTPs| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|No TTPs| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites
items.description: Threat actors are selling investment scam templates created using the legitimate DCloud Uni-App toolkit. The post Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The DCloud Uni-App toolkit is being exploited by threat actors to create investment scam templates, affecting over 200,000 scam sites. The technical details of this vulnerability are not explicitly stated, but it is clear that the legitimate toolkit is being misused. If exploited, this could lead to significant financial losses for individuals and organizations, with the potential for widespread impact due to the large number of scam sites. To remediate this, defenders should be cautious of suspicious investment opportunities and verify the legitimacy of websites before engaging with them. Additionally, monitoring for unusual network activity and implementing robust security measures, such as firewalls and intrusion detection systems, can help prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic

items.title: Third-Party Breaches Teach Education Sector a Costly Lesson in Vendor Risk
items.description: Rising threats from third-party actors are forcing institutions to play defense to protect student data from ransomware and other attacks.
items.ai_summary: The education sector is facing a significant threat from third-party breaches, which can lead to ransomware attacks and data encryption, as evidenced by the detection of T1486, a tactic used for data encryption for impact. This attack vector is particularly concerning as it can result in the loss of sensitive student data, causing significant financial and reputational damage to institutions. To mitigate this risk, educational institutions must conduct thorough risk assessments of their third-party vendors, ensuring they have robust security controls in place, such as multi-factor authentication and regular software updates. Specifically, institutions should require vendors to implement encryption protocols, such as TLS 1.2 or higher, and to maintain up-to-date systems, including the latest versions of operating systems and applications. By taking these proactive measures, educational institutions can reduce their vulnerability to third-party breaches and protect sensitive student data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1486| B[Impact]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FBI: Russian hackers now target Signal backup recovery keys
items.description: The FBI and CISA are warning that a phishing campaign targeting Signal users tied to Russian intelligence services has evolved to steal Signal Backup Recovery Keys, allowing attackers to access victims' historical messages. [...]

items.title: CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is giving federal agencies until Sunday to patch a vulnerability in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server that is being actively exploited. [...]

items.title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
items.description: The FBI and CISA have updated their March warning about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key. Hand it over once, and the attacker can restore the account's backup, read the private and group message history, and take over the account. Worse, the key keeps working.

items.title: AI Decline? Confidence in Autonomous Penetration Testing Falls
items.description: Companies are still experimenting with automated AI systems to find security weaknesses, but fewer are relying on the technology.

items.title: New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks
items.description: A newly discovered cyber attack campaign has been observed delivering a previously undocumented malware family called SharkLoader that acts as a loader for deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised hosts. Kaspersky, which is tracking the activity under the moniker StrikeShark, said the campaign has targeted a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan,

items.title: Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack
items.description: Polymarket says it will fully reimburse customers who lost an estimated $3 million after hackers injected a malicious script into the platform's frontend following a breach at a third-party vendor. [...]

items.title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites
items.description: Threat actors are creating OpenAI tenants that impersonate legitimate companies and inviting employees to join them, in what appears to be a ploy to trick targets into submitting sensitive company information in chats and projects. [...]

items.title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
items.description: Cisco joins a growing list of security platform providers that are betting that securing the agentic workforce means turning identity into the primary control plane.

items.title: New Initiative Tackles Security for End-of-Life Open Source Software
items.description: The Open Source Sustainability Initiative's goal is to help enterprises manage and secure aging open source projects while maintaining regulatory compliance.

items.title: AI Won't Wipe-Out Entry-Level Cybersecurity Jobs
items.description: Instead of eliminating jobs for early-career cyber pros, AI is creating new opportunities for candidates with strong human decision-making skills.

items.title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
items.description: AWS has patched the vulnerability and published its own advisory to inform customers about the potential impact. The post Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: More Klue Breach Victims Identified as Hackers Get Hacked
items.description: Roughly two dozen companies have notified their customers of the Klue-Salesforce incident impact. The post More Klue Breach Victims Identified as Hackers Get Hacked appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough
items.description: AI won't replace GRC analysts, but it can eliminate much of the repetitive work they do. Anecdotes walks through building an agent that continuously monitors controls, identifies evidence gaps, and opens remediation tasks. [...]

items.title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
items.description: A flaw in the Linux kernel's traffic-control subsystem can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems. CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed "pedit COW," is an out-of-bounds write in the packet-editing action (act_pedit) that corrupts shared page-cache memory. A public, working exploit appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16. Red Hat rates the flaw as

items.title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
items.description: A high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer let a malicious repository run commands and steal a developer's cloud credentials. The path was short: a developer opens the repo, trusts the workspace, and Amazon Q does the rest. Amazon has patched it. Tracked as CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5), the bug sat in how Amazon's AI coding assistant handled Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Wiz

items.title: Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store
items.description: Apple removed VK's flagship social network VKontakte, often described as Russia's equivalent of Facebook, along with VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki and Mail.ru services, including its email application.

items.title: Turla group adds more malware to Russia’s espionage efforts against Ukraine
items.description: Threat intelligence researchers at Google described StockStay, the latest malware developed by the Russian cyber-espionage group known as Turla.

items.title: Meeting Trump's 2030 Quantum Deadline Will be Expensive, Complex
items.description: Getting accurate visibility into IT and OT systems will be compounded by multivendor environments, misaligned update life cycles, and interoperability gaps.

items.title: Russia used social engineering to breach prominent messaging accounts, Ukraine says
items.description: Ukraine's SBU described a long-running Russian operation that used fake tech-support workers to persuade people to hand over credentials to their messaging apps.

items.title: Thanks for Crushing the Submissions Inbox. We're Trying to Keep Up
items.description: It might be taking a bit longer than usual to respond to your submissions — here's why.

items.title: Nebulock Raises $25 Million for AI-Native Contextual Security
items.description: The cybersecurity startup provides threat hunting, proactive detection, and behavioral security analytics. The post Nebulock Raises $25 Million for AI-Native Contextual Security appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday added a critical remote code execution vulnerability impacting PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM enterprise Product Data Management (PDM) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) software to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability in question is

items.title: FCC votes to toughen rules in bid to better protect undersea cables
items.description: In an unprecedented move, the FCC also said it plans to mandate that owners and operators of submarine line terminal equipment (SLTE) be licensed.

items.title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
items.description: DirtyClone is a new Linux kernel privilege escalation in the DirtyFrag family. JFrog Security Research published a working exploit walkthrough for the flaw on June 25, the first public demonstration for this variant. Tracked as CVE-2026-43503 (CVSS 8.8), it lets a local user corrupt file-backed memory through a cloned network packet and gain root. The patch landed in

items.title: Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance
items.description: AI agents are moving through enterprise environments, inheriting permissions, traversing systems, and executing decisions at machine speed with minimal oversight. The identity infrastructure built to govern human access wasn't designed for autonomous actors, and the gap between what enterprises are deploying and what their governance programs actually cover is widening fast. This guide breaks

items.title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
items.description: It will provide the tools and channels to report, patch, and disclose open source software vulnerabilities. The post Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
items.description: The decentralized prediction market said hackers targeted some of its users through a compromise of a third-party vendor. The post $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
items.description: Turla has been using the backdoor against government and military organizations in Ukraine for espionage. The post Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
items.description: CISA has added the remote code execution flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The post First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
items.description: A major overhaul of the Model Context Protocol shifts critical security responsibilities from the protocol itself to developers and platform operators. The post New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic is testing desktop-like Claude Cowork for mobile
items.description: Anthropic appears to be testing Claude Cowork support on mobile, allowing you to manage long-running Claude tasks from your phone. [...]

items.title: Robinhood Cuts Access Approval Time to Support High-Velocity Development
items.description: The fintech company's engineering-first application security team reengineered the process for granting system access, making it easier and more secure for developers working on their projects. Here are the lessons learned from Robinhood's experience.

items.title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
items.description: Authorities in Poland have arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of breaching telecommunications partners and hijacking email accounts to carry out SIM-swapping attacks. [...]

items.title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
items.description: The flaw enables server-side request forgery (SSRF) and escalates privileges to root, impacting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME deployments.

items.title: Russian APT 'Gamaredon' Upgrades Its Arsenal, Requiring New Defenses
items.description: The FSB state-sponsored operation has gotten a lot better at loading its malware and hiding its servers.

items.title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly abusing Shop, the order-tracking app from Shopify, by adding fake purchase receipts in users' order histories to trick them into providing sensitive data or installing remote access software. [...]

items.title: DHS chief says president has met with likely CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600
items.description: Once a new CISA director is in place, the agency will ramp up hiring efforts, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told lawmakers. The White House has not yet announced a nominee.

items.title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, allowing enrolled devices to continue receiving security updates until October 12, 2027. [...]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
